text:
YALE LAW SCHOOL

Public Law & Legal Theory
           Research Paper Series
                 Research Paper No. 64

The Two Western Cultures of Privacy:
         Dignity versus Liberty

by

James Q. Whitman

This paper can be downloaded without charge from the
 Social Science Research Network Paper Collection at
       http://papers.ssrn.com/abstract=476041
                                                     I

In every corner of the western world, writers proclaim “privacy” as a supremely

important human good, as a value somehow at the core of what makes life worth living.

Without our privacy, we lose “our very integrity as persons,” Charles Fried declared

thirty years ago. 1 Many others have agreed since that privacy is somehow fundamental to

our “personhood.”2 It is a commonplace, moreover, that our privacy is peculiarly

menaced by the evolution of modern society, with its burgeoning technologies of

surveillance and inquiry. Commentators paint this menace in very dark colors: Invasions

of our privacy are said to portend a society of “horror,”3 to “injure [us] in [our] very

humanity,”4 or even to threaten “totalitarianism,”5 and the establishment of law

protecting privacy is accordingly declared to be a universal obligation of civilized

society. It is the rare privacy advocate who resists citing Orwell when describing these

dangers.

At the same time, honest advocates of “privacy” protections are forced to admit

that the concept of “privacy” is embarrassingly difficult to define. 6 “Nobody,” writes

Judith Jarvis Thomson dryly, “seems to have any very clear idea what it is.”7 Not every

1
   Fried, Privacy, in Ferdinand David Schoeman, Philosophical Dimensions of Privacy: An Anthology
(Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1984), 205.
2
  See especially Jeffrey H. Reiman, Privacy, Intimacy and Personhood, in Schoeman, ed., Philosophical
Dimensions of Privacy, 300-316. For more recent examples of this widespread idea, e.g. Hugh Miller, III,
DNA Blueprints, Personhood, and Genetic Privacy, 8 Health Matrix 179 (1998); Jonathan Kahn, Privacy
as a Legal Principle of Identity Maintenance, 33 Seton Hall L. Rev. 371 (2003). There are of course other
approaches, and in particular more skeptical ones, such as those offered by some feminists. See e.g. Sally
F. Goldfarb, Violence Against Women and the Persistence of Privacy, 61 Ohio St. L. J. 1 (2000).
3
   Edward J. Eberle, The Right To Information Self-Determination, 2001 Utah L. Rev. 965, 995.
4
   Fried, Privacy, 202.
5
  Jed Rubenfeld, The Right of Privacy, 102 Harv. L. Rev. 737 (1989).
6
   E.g. Robert Post, Three Concepts of Privacy, 89 Geo. L. J., 2087 (2000); Willam M. Beaney, The Right to
Privacy and American Law, 31 L. & Contemp. Probs. 253, 255 (1966).
7
   Thomson, The Right to Privacy, in Schoeman, ed., Philosophical Dimensions of Privacy, 272.
 author is as skeptical as Thomson, but many of them feel obliged to concede that

“privacy,” fundamentally important though it may be, is an unusually slippery concept.
